name: Tepache
subtitle: Mexico’s pre-Columbian pineapple fizzer
blrNote: Pineapples are ₹60–80 year-round at any local pushcart. Use Mandya jaggery (Bella) instead of Mexican piloncillo—it’s functionally identical and adds a gorgeous mineral depth.

ingredients.name: Pineapple skin & core
substitutions: Organic is better to ensure wild yeast

IN.where: Any fruit stall — keep the scraps after eating the fruit

tldr: Pineapple scraps + jaggery + water. Wait 2 days. Drink the sun.

steps.title: Prep the scraps
steps.instruction: Wash the pineapple exterior lightly (don’t scrub off the yeast). Remove the skin in large strips and keep the core. Eat the flesh.

steps.title: Dissolve & Mix
steps.instruction: Dissolve jaggery in 2L water. Add the skins, core, and cinnamon stick to the jar.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: Cover with cloth. At BLR’s 25°C, it will bubble aggressively in 24h. Skim the white foam daily.
steps.checkpoint: Smells like fermented honey and pineapple

steps.title: Bottle & Chill
steps.instruction: Strain and bottle. Refrigerate immediately. If left out, it becomes pineapple vinegar (also useful!).

culturalContext.story: Tepache dates back to Pre-Columbian Mexico. Originally made with corn (tepatli), it evolved into the pineapple version we know today. In Mexico City, "Tepacherías" serve it cold in plastic bags with a straw.
culturalContext.significance: It is the ultimate zero-waste ferment. You pay for the fruit, but the "drink" is made from what usually goes in the bin.
culturalContext.funFact: In some parts of Mexico, a pinch of salt and chilli powder is added to the glass before serving.

thingsToAccountFor.title: The Explosive Factor
thingsToAccountFor.description: At 28°C+ in Bengaluru, carbonation builds FAST. If you bottle this, "burp" the bottles twice a day or they will explode.

dehydratorIntegration.result: Fermented pineapple "leather" bits—great for infusing into spirits.

variations.name: Tepache con Chile
variations.description: Add one slit green chilli during fermentation for a spicy kick.
